english: gu-ma (gu ma) Dzl.; sdú-ge-ba (sdu ge ba) Cs.: 'the state of being somewhat pleasing'(?); in a prayer occurs: bod-báṅs sdúg-ge sñiṅ-re-rje (bod 'bangs sdug ge snying re rje) the good, poor Tibetans, just as in W. *sdug-pa-tsé* (*sdug pa tse*) is used; often (but not necessarily) rather pityingly: ḱo sdug-pa-tsé (kho sdug pa tse) the good man (will do his utmost); *rí-pa sdug-pa-tsé* (*ri pa sdug pa tse*) the good fieldmouse (speedily made off); but also: *sab dug-pa-tsé ắ-lu żig toṅ* (*sab dug pa tse a lu zhig tong*) W. good sir, give me a few potatoes! II. vb. to be oppressed, afflicted, grieved, like gduṅ-ba (gdung ba), sems las-kyis sdug-nas (sems las kyis sdug nas) by sorrow Mil.; *sem máṅ-po máṅ-po dug soṅ* (*sem mang po mang po dug song*) C. I was very, very sorry for it; . . . pas sdug-go (. . . pas sdug go) we are miserable, because . . . Dzl.; sdug-par gyúr-ba (sdug par gyur ba) to become unhappy, to get into distress Dzl. III. sbst., affliction, misery, distress, bod sdug-pai mgo dzugs (bod sdug pai mgo dzugs) that is the beginning of the misfortunes of Tibet Ma.; ṅed-la sdug-pai re-mos bab (nged la sdug pai re mos bab) (then) came our turn of being visited by affliction Mil.; more frq. sdug (sdug), and sdug-bsṅál (sdug bsngal) (v. below) sdúg-tu mi yoṅ dug-gam (sdug tu mi yong dug gam) are you not in distress? Mil.; sdug ḱur byed-pa (sdug khur byed pa) to undergo hardships (voluntarily), to bear affliction (patiently), to suffer, in an emphatical sense, Mil.; sdug mi teg (sdug mi theg) you cannot endure the hardships Mil.; *ka-dúg máṅ-po jhe'-pa* (*ka dug mang po jhe' pa*) C. to work hard, to drudge; skyid-sdug (skyid sdug) good and adverse fortune, good luck and ill luck, very frq.; bde-sdug (bde sdug) id.; sdug-sogs byed-pa (sdug sogs byed pa) (the contrary to tsogs-sogs byed-pa (tshogs sogs byed pa)) to accumulate misery upon one's self Mil.; *dug maṅ-po taṅ-wa* (*dug mang po tang wa*) C. to plague or vex a good deal, to inflict injury, c. la (la); gźan-sdug-gi sdig-pa (gzhan sdug gi sdig pa) the sin of having done evil to others Mil.; *dug zo'-la taṅ-wa* (*dug zo' la tang wa*) C. to torture, to put to the rack; sdug bab-pa (sdug bab pa) to be in mourning Cs.; sdug sraṅ-ba (sdug srang ba) to mourn Cs.; sdug-caṅ (sdug cang) col. fatiguing, worrying. — sdug (sdug) as adj., unhappy, miserable, Pth., is of rare occurrence. Comp. and deriv. sdug-ḱaṅ (sdug khang) a chamber of mourning, a darkened room Cs. — sdug-gos (sdug gos) a mourning dress Cs. — sdug-bsṅál (sdug bsngal) the most frq. word for misfortune, misery, suffering; also pain, sdug-bsṅál-gyis gduṅs-pa (sdug bsngal gyis gdungs pa) Dzl., sdug-bsṅál myoṅ-ba (sdug bsngal myong ba) (W. *toṅ-ce* (*tong ce*)) to be in calamity, to suffer pain; *dug-ṅál toṅ-wa (dug ngal tong wa), tér-wa* (*ter wa*) C. (*taṅ-ce* (*tang ce*) W.), to inflict pain, to grieve, to torment; sdug-bsṅál daṅ ldan-pa (sdug bsngal dang ldan pa), sdug-bsṅál-can (sdug bsngal can) unhappy, miserable; misery, distress, affliction; *dug-ṅál jhẻ-pa* (*dug ngal jhe pa*) C. to lament, wail, moan; sdug-bsṅál-du gyúr-ba (sdug bsngal du gyur ba) to become sorrowful or melancholy; *ṅá-la ná-ga-ri ma sès-pe dug-ṅál yod* (*nga la na ga ri ma shes pe dug ngal yod*) Ld. I regret my not knowing Sanskrit; sdug-bsṅál-ba (sdug bsngal ba) (vb.) to be unhappy, (sbst.) the state of unhappiness, Thgy.; sdug-bsṅál-bai skad (sdug bsngal bai skad) lamentable, doleful cries. — sdug-mtug (sdug mthug) C. accumulating calamity. — sdug-dré (sdug 'dre) a demon Sch. — *dúg-po* (*dug po*) C. wretched (road), savage (dog), ill-bred, naughty, unamiable; evil (sbst.), dúg-po byed-pa (dug po byed pa) to do evil Mil.; *mi-la dúg-po taṅ-wa* (*mi la dug po tang wa*) C. to do evil to a person, to molest, trouble, annoy, injure, a person. — sdug-poṅs-pa (sdug phongs pa) Stg., C., poor. — sdug-źwa (sdug zhwa) a mourning-hood Cs. — sdug-sraṅ (sdug srang) inured to hardships; the being hardened Mil.
1. Sch.: the folds of a garment; sdúd-ḱa (sdud kha) string for drawing together the opening of a bag, drawing-hem. — 2. Cs. synthesis, byed-sdúd (byed sdud) analysis and synthesis.

pf. bsdus (bsdus), fut. and likewise for the pres. tense) bsdu (bsdu), imp. sdus (sdus), bsdu (bsdu), vb. a. to °dú-ba (°du ba), 1. to collect, gather, lay up, amass, assemble, riches, flowers, broken victuals, taxes, crops, earnings, men, cattle etc., frq.; to put together, to compile, miṅ-rnams . . . nas bsdus (ming rnams . . . nas bsdus) the names have been put together out of . . . Glr.; to brush or sweep together, W.: *ḱyim-sa äl-mo-ne (or daṅ)* (*khyim sa al mo ne (or dang)*) the dust with a broom; dbaṅ-du (dbang du) to subject, subdue, frq. — 2. to unite, join, combine, śiṅ gsum mgo (shing gsum mgo) three pieces of wood at their upper ends Dzl.; six kingdoms into one Dzl. (to join) actions, words, and thoughts in the path of virtue Dzl.; dmag-rnams ḱór-du (dmag rnams khor du) (joining) the troops with his retinue Dzl.; ḱyo-śug-tu (khyo shug tu) to unite in matrimony, to give in marriage. — 3. to condense, to comprise, all moral precepts in three main points, the letters of the alphabet in five classes Gram.; esp. with nyúṅ-ṅur (nyung ngur),
